 Arthur Conan Doyle

Type: introduction
Title: The Setting and Premises
Summary: Watson introduces the context of a scandal at a university town in 1895, promising discretion to protect the reputation of those involved.
Content: It was in the year '95 that a combination of events, into which I
 need not enter, caused Mr. Sherlock Holmes and myself to spend some
 weeks in one of our great University towns, and it was during this
 time that the small but instructive adventure which I am about to
 relate befell us...
Keywords: 1895, University, scandal
Topics: Detective Fiction
People: Sherlock Holmes, Watson
Locations: University town
Organizations: 
Questions: What is the nature of the scandal?

Type: background
Title: The Arrival of Mr. Soames
Summary: Holmes and Watson are interrupted by Hilton Soames, a distressed tutor from St. Luke's College.
Content: We were residing at the time in furnished lodgings close to a library
 where Sherlock Holmes was pursuing some laborious researches in early
 English charters... Here it was that one evening we received a visit from an acquaintance, Mr. Hilton Soames, tutor and lecturer at the College of St. Luke's.
Keywords: Hilton Soames, St. Luke's
Topics: Mystery
People: Sherlock Holmes, Watson, Hilton Soames
Locations: 
Organizations: College of St. Luke's
Questions: Why is Soames so agitated?

Type: argument
Title: Soames's Request
Summary: Soames asks Holmes for help with a sensitive incident at his college, hoping to avoid police involvement.
Content: "I trust, Mr. Holmes, that you can spare me a few hours of your
 valuable time. We have had a very painful incident at St. Luke's..."
Keywords: scandal, help, St. Luke's
Topics: Mystery
People: Sherlock Holmes, Soames
Locations: 
Organizations: St. Luke's
Questions: What is the 'painful incident'?

Type: argument
Title: Maintaining Discretion
Summary: Soames emphasizes the necessity of discretion to protect the college's reputation.
Content: "No, no, my dear sir; such a course is utterly impossible. When once
 the law is evoked it cannot be stayed again..."
Keywords: discretion, scandal
Topics: Ethics
People: Holmes, Soames
Locations: 
Organizations: college

Title: Holmes Accepts

 congenial surroundings of Baker Street... He shrugged his shoulders in ungracious acquiescence...

Type: background
Title: The Fortescue Scholarship
Summary: Soames explains that as an examiner for the prestigious Fortescue Scholarship, he is responsible for keeping the secret Greek translation paper safe.

 of the examination for the Fortescue Scholarship..."
Keywords: Fortescue Scholarship, examination, Greek, proofs
Topics: Education

Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions: Were the papers stolen?

Type: data
Title: Leaving the Proofs
Summary: Soames describes leaving the exam proofs unattended for over an hour.
Content: "To-day about three o'clock the proofs of this paper arrived from the
 printers... At four-thirty my task was not yet completed. I had,
 however, promised to take tea... so I left the proof upon my desk."
Keywords: proofs, printer, tea
Topics: Mystery
People: Soames
Locations: 
Organizations:

Type: evidence
Title: The Key in the Door
Summary: Soames discovers his servant Bannister's key left in the lock, implying someone might have entered the room while he was away.
Content: "You are aware, Mr. Holmes, that our college doors are double... As I approached
 my outer door I was amazed to see a key in it... The only duplicate... was that which belonged to my servant, Bannister..."
Keywords: key, Bannister, door

Title: Evidence of Disturbance
Summary: Soames notices the exam papers were moved and tampered with.

Type: evidence
Title: The Height Clue
Summary: Holmes explains that he measured the height required to see the papers from the window, which implicated the taller students.
Content: "When I approached your room I measured how tall
 a man would need to be in order to see as he passed what papers were
 on the central table. I am six feet high... No one less than that would have a chance."
Keywords: height, window, papers
Topics: Deduction
People: Holmes
Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions:

Type: argument
Title: The Jumping Connection
Summary: Holmes realizes that Gilchrist's identity as a long-distance jumper explains the physical traces left on the desk.
Content: "I could make nothing [of the centre table], until in
 your description of Gilchrist you mentioned that he was a
 long-distance jumper. Then the whole thing came to me in an instant..."
Keywords: jumper, Gilchrist, evidence
Topics: Deduction
People: Holmes, Soames, Gilchrist
Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions:

Type: argument
Title: Gilchrist's Impulse
Summary: Holmes explains that Gilchrist acted on impulse after seeing the papers while holding his spiked jumping shoes.
Content: "What happened was this. This young fellow had employed his afternoon
 at the athletic grounds... As he passed your window he saw... these proofs... A sudden impulse came over
 him to enter..."
Keywords: impulse, shoes, papers
Topics: Deduction
People: Gilchrist, Soames
Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions:

Type: evidence
Title: The Evidence Explained
Summary: Holmes links the gloves, the clay, and the spikes to Gilchrist's shoes, solving the case.
Content: "He thought the tutor must return by the main gate... Suddenly he heard him
 at the very door... He forgot his gloves... I walked out to the athletic grounds... saw that tenacious black clay..."
Keywords: gloves, clay, shoes, spikes
Topics: Forensics
People: Holmes, Gilchrist
Locations: athletic grounds
Organizations: 
Questions:

Type: conclusion
Title: The Culprit Admits
Summary: Gilchrist admits the truth.
Content: The student had drawn himself erect. "Yes, sir, it is true," said he. "Good heavens, have you nothing to add?" cried Soames.
Keywords: admission, Gilchrist
Topics: Ethics
People: Gilchrist, Soames
Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions: What else does Gilchrist have to add?

Type: conclusion
Title: A Change of Plan
Summary: Gilchrist reveals he had already decided not to take the exam and is instead joining the Rhodesian Police.
Content: "Yes, sir, I have... I have a letter here... I have determined not to go in for the examination. I
 have been offered a commission in the Rhodesian Police..."
Keywords: letter, Rhodesian Police
Topics: Ethics
People: Gilchrist, Soames
Locations: South Africa
Organizations: Rhodesian Police
Questions:

Type: conclusion
Title: Bannister's Role
Summary: Gilchrist credits Bannister with guiding him toward the correct moral path.
Content: "I am indeed pleased... But why did you change your
 purpose?"
 Gilchrist pointed to Bannister. "There is the man who set me in the right path."
Keywords: Bannister, morality
Topics: Ethics
People: Gilchrist, Bannister
Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions:

Type: conclusion
Title: Bannister's Motivation
Summary: Bannister explains his deep, long-standing loyalty to Gilchrist's family, which motivated him to protect the young man from his mistake.
Content: "It was simple enough... I was butler to old Sir Jabez Gilchrist... When he was ruined I came to the college... I
 watched his son all I could for the sake of the old days."
Keywords: loyalty, Butler, Sir Jabez
Topics: Characters
People: Bannister, Sir Jabez Gilchrist, Gilchrist
Locations: 
Organizations: 
Questions:

Type: conclusion
Title: Resolution
Summary: Holmes concludes the case, expresses approval of the resolution, and departs with Watson.
Content: "No, indeed," said Holmes, heartily... "Well,
 Soames, I think we have cleared your little problem up... I trust
 that a bright future awaits you in Rhodesia."
Keywords: resolution, Holmes, Watson
Topics: Mystery
People: Holmes, Soames, Watson, Gilchrist
